dialogue.utterance: Code one for a possible seizure at a residence. Estimated time of arrival three minutes.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Our mother is having a seizure again.
I'm having convulsions in my left arm and face, and I feel weak. I also have a headache and nausea.
Can you tell me your name and what day it is today?
My name is Susan, and I think it's the 30th.
I see your eyes are opening but showing jerking movements. Does your left side feel weaker or not under control?
Yes, my left side more. I can't feel my hand properly.
I can see your airway is open and you're taking your own breath with some difficulty.
Ma'am, we're going to be checking your breathing now.
Your respirations are 34 per minute, which is elevated. We will monitor closely.
Pulse is strong and central pulses are present. Skin is warm, dry, and intact. Blood pressure is elevated at 164 over 86.
Skin is intact without signs of trauma or abnormalities. Have you noticed anything on your skin recently?
Pulse is 89 beats per minute. We will recheck shortly.
Blood pressure: 164 over 86. This aligns with our prior assessment.
Glucose is 130 milligrams per deciliter. Holding steady for now.
And oxygen saturation is 91 percent. We'll check again as needed.
Cardiac monitor shows a normal sinus rhythm. I'm attaching for 12-lead EKG next.
We're going to place an IV access in your arm to administer medication and monitor for the seizure activity.
We're placing you on a cardiac monitor now to assess for any cardiac concerns related to the episode.
CV ECG 4 lead monitoring for continuous observation while we gather further data.
Taking a 12-lead EKG to evaluate for any abnormalities that may relate to your symptoms today.
May I ask your age and your estimated weight?
I'm 41 years old and I had a headache and felt nauseated all morning. I also had a seizure this morning earlier, but it stopped and then came back again.
Mother had a stroke in the past, leaving her with left arm contracture and speech difficulties.
She also has epilepsy from before. She ran out of her Keppra and was due to get more when the pharmacy opened today.
Mother, was there any time you remembered eating or drinking recently before today's episode?
I don't recall the last time I had something. It's hard to remember now because I'm dizzy and tired.
Pupils are equal at 4 mm each. Do you notice your eyes moving unnaturally or in a pattern?
Yes, like they are dancing back and forth to the left side - feels constant, actually.
Trachea is midline. I note no jugular venous distension, which suggests stable venous return back to the heart.
Your skin is intact and warm. You have no signs of rashes, discoloration, or swelling at this time. Any difficulty feeling or moving parts of your body recently?
Chest wall is intact. Tachypnea is present. Patient has not yet been moved, so we're delaying full respiratory exam for now. We'll reassess later if needed.
Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without distension. Pelvis appears stable at this time. Any discharges or abdominal pain today, Susan?
Your extremities are intact with pulses present in all four limbs, and the left arm appears contracted. Are you experiencing any pain or numbness in them currently?.
Your extremities show +PMSx4. Any swelling or bruising?.
Your back is atraumatic. No signs of trauma or lacerations observed at present. Any pain in the back or spine?.
I'm noting a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15: you're alert and responding well. I don't see any cognitive impairment at the moment.
How would you describe the sensation from the convulsions you experienced earlier?.
It feels like a contraction or tightening. Not like a spasm, more like a repetitive muscle jerk.
On a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain you've ever felt in your life, how would you describe this discomfort?.
A 7 for sure. Not the worst-but definitely interfering with daily function.
Respirations have improved to 24. I'm observing decreased breath effort at this point.
Now, your oxygen saturation is at 97 percent, and we note an improvement in your breathing.
Pulse is 88. We've not seen a significant drop in heart rate, but we are monitoring you very closely.
Blood pressure is 158 over 93. Still elevated, but manageable at this time.
Glucose is currently not measured at this time. Let's move forward with medication administration.
Giving first dose of Midazolam (Versed) to help calm the seizure activity. This is within our standing order protocol.
Pulse is now 114, and respirations have gone up again to 30. I'm observing sinus tachycardia now.
Blood pressure is 153 over 100 and oxygen saturation at 94. Cardiac rhythm now shows sinus tachycardia - we'll reassess following the second dose.
Giving second dose of Midazolam (Versed). Patient is not allergic to this medication (NKA) as noted in her history.
Pulse has dropped slightly to 109, and respirations are 26. Blood pressure is at 125 over 85, and oxygen sat is improved to 98.
Respiratory rate is currently measured at 26 per minute. Oxygen saturation is 94 percent at this time.
VCU Medical Center has been contacted via cell phone for incoming patient. Facility is aware of the situation.
You are capable of airway protection and are classified as no known allergies (NKA).
You have left-sided hemiplegia post-stroke from your previous CVA. We'll take care to avoid placing pressure on that side, especially during stretcher transfers.
You're also classified as CA+Ox4. We'll bring you up slowly and monitor breathing the entire way.
Can you stand and pivot with assistance? We'll transfer you to the stretcher in the semi-Fowler's position and secure you for transport.
Yes, but it's hard. Help me please.
Standing up now, we'll pivot and transfer you to the stretcher with assistance.
Now transported to stretcher. We are securing you with five seat belts to prevent movement during transport.
Remaining in semi-Fowler's position for airway stability and comfort.
All secured for transport.
You have a history of status epilepticus, currently undergoing frequent monitoring.
Respiratory rate is now 26 per minute. Oxygen saturation is 94 percent at this time.
We're following protocol 3-12 for medical - seizure management with standing order midazolam.
Oxygen saturation is now at 98 percent, and your respirations are remaining relatively stable.
During hospital transfer, we'll use a draw sheet pull to assist in safely transferring you to the ER bed using minimal movement to avoid seizure triggers.
You will be moved from ambulance to emergency room. We are aware of the ED bed and will inform personnel upon arrival.
